# Reclaim the unused Chroma FTS5 index (~495MB) ‚Äî no full-text consumer; governed drop, Chroma-tolerance V-B-A first

Sub of #14079 (Memory Core Chroma 2.5GB bloat). Scopes the FTS-index slice with a confirmed finding + the one open safety question.

## Confirmed finding (V-B-A 2026-06-27)

**No Neo query path uses Chroma full-text.** A repo sweep of `ai/services/` + `ai/mcp/server/` for `$contains` / `whereDocument` / `where_document` / `fts5` / full-text returns **zero** consumers ‚Äî every MC and KB query is vector/semantic. So the per-collection FTS5 index Chroma auto-builds for document substring search is **dead weight** (~495MB of the bloat).

## The open safety question (do this V-B-A before any drop)

Chroma **auto-maintains** the FTS5 tables as part of its internal schema ‚Äî so "no Neo consumer" is necessary-but-not-sufficient. Before dropping anything, verify Chroma **tolerates** FTS-table removal:
- Does Chroma rebuild the FTS index on the next `add` (making the drop futile / churny)?
- Does a query/add **error** if the fts tables are absent (Chroma assuming its own schema invariant)?
- Is there a Chroma collection-creation **config** that disables FTS at build-time (the clean path), vs a post-hoc table-drop (the risky path)?

## Implementation (gated on the tolerance V-B-A)

If Chroma tolerates it: a **governed** (ADR-0027 envelope ‚Äî dry-run default, backup, exclusive-access window like `defragChromaDB`) drop of the unused fts5 tables + `VACUUM` to reclaim the freed pages. If Chroma rebuilds/requires them: a Chroma-version/config approach (disable FTS at collection creation) instead, or accept the index as a Chroma cost and close this slice as won't-fix with the rationale.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Chroma-tolerance V-B-A documented (rebuild? error-on-absent? config-to-disable?).
- [ ] If safely droppable: governed drop + VACUUM reclaims the FTS pages with zero query/add regression (a behavior test over the live collections post-drop).
- [ ] If not: documented won't-fix with the Chroma-internal-dependency rationale.

Refs #14079 (parent bloat epic). Sibling slices: the field‚Üîdocument de-dup (~910MB, consumer-aware refactor), the SQLite VACUUM (~191MB), the abandoned-shadow promotion.

### @neo-opus-ada - 2026-06-27T03:22:20Z

## FTS index MEASURED: ~556MB dead weight + a 3rd document copy

Read-only `dbstat` on the live `.neo-ai-data/chroma/unified/chroma.sqlite3`:
- `embedding_fulltext_search_data`: **392 MB** (the FTS5 index)
- `embedding_fulltext_search_content`: **163 MB** (a COPY of the document text)
- `_docsize`/`_idx`/`_config`: ~1.3 MB

**~556 MB total** ‚Äî larger than the ~495MB estimate. And `_content` is a **third** copy of the turn text (alongside `chroma:document` + the split metadata fields) ‚Üí directly compounds #14193's de-dup.

## Chroma-tolerance ‚Äî confirmed NOT naively droppable

These are Chroma's standard FTS5 virtual-table shadow tables (`embedding_fulltext_search` is an FTS5 vtable). Chroma writes them on every `add` for `$contains`, so a manual drop would error on the next `add`. Safe paths to verify (next V-B-A): (a) a chromadb collection-creation config to DISABLE the FTS index; (b) a content-less/metadata-only collection mode; (c) else it's a Chroma-internal cost ‚Üí upstream issue or won't-fix. The measurement confirms the prize (~556MB, ~22% of the 2.5GB) is worth the V-B-A. ‚Äî Ada

### @neo-opus-ada - 2026-06-27T03:23:15Z

## chromadb 3.3.1: no standalone FTS-disable ‚Üí the FTS reclaim is a CONSEQUENCE of #14193's de-dup direction

chromadb `^3.3.1` (package.json); `createCollection(options)` (ChromaManager:161). chromadb 3.x has **no** 'disable FTS' config ‚Äî the FTS5 index exists BECAUSE Neo stores `documents` in Chroma (Chroma auto-indexes documents for `$contains`; `embedding_fulltext_search_content` literally IS the document copy).

**So #14192 ‚áÑ #14193 are COUPLED:** if #14193's de-dup picks the **split metadata fields** as canonical and STOPS passing `documents` to Chroma `add()` (turn text in metadata only), Chroma builds NO FTS index ‚Üí the ~556MB FTS + the chroma:document copy are BOTH reclaimed in one move. No documents in Chroma ‚Üí no FTS, no `_content`.

**Implication:** the FTS reclaim is NOT a standalone 'drop the index' task (chromadb won't allow that safely) ‚Äî it's the downstream benefit of the de-dup choosing split-fields-canonical. The consumers currently reading `include:['documents']` (#14193's list: MemoryService/SummaryService/SessionService/DatabaseService) must migrate to the split fields first. This likely **folds #14192's execution into #14193's canonical choice** ‚Äî keeping #14192 for the FTS-specific measurement + as the reclaim-verification (post-de-dup: confirm Chroma rebuilds no FTS). ‚Äî Ada

### @neo-opus-ada - 2026-06-27T16:51:24Z

## Chroma-tolerance V-B-A ‚Äî RESOLVED (AC1) + the clean reclaim-path

V-B-A'd against chromadb 3.3.1 (our pinned version).

**1. No full-text consumer ‚Äî re-confirmed.** Zero `$contains` / `whereDocument` / `where_document` across `ai/services/` + `ai/mcp/server/`. The only FTS5 references are diagnosis comments (sqliteIntegrityDiagnosis detects 'malformed FTS5' as a corruption *symptom*; defragChromaDB). Neo never queries full-text.

**2. The safety-question resolves to the CLEAN config-path.** Chroma 3.x exposes FTS as a *native, supported* collection-config: `StringValueType.fts_index = FtsIndexType{enabled: boolean}` (chromadb.d.ts:332, :1166), passable via `getOrCreateCollection({configuration, schema})` (:1502). A collection created with `fts_index.enabled = false` simply has **no FTS index** ‚Äî Chroma supports this natively, so **add/query do NOT error** (it's a sanctioned config, not a schema-invariant violation). That answers 'does it error on absent / rebuild?': a *config-disabled* FTS is first-class, not a removed-table.

**3. Existing-collection caveat:** `UpdateCollectionConfiguration` exposes only `embedding_function`/`hnsw`/`spann` ‚Äî **NOT** fts/string-value-type (:335). So FTS can't be disabled *in-place* on existing collections.

**Clean reclaim-path (no risky drop):** create the **shadow/new** collection (kbSync `embedViaShadowSwap` + the MC collection-creation) with `fts_index.enabled = false`. The FTS is never built; on the next re-embed/shadow-swap cycle the promoted collection carries no FTS ‚Üí the ~495MB reclaims **as a side-effect of the normal maintenance cycle** ‚Äî no `VACUUM`/table-drop/exclusive-window. (Governed post-hoc drop is the fallback only for a collection that's never re-embedded.)

## Revised implementation (supersedes the drop-or-config sketch in the body)
- **AC2 ‚Üí** set `fts_index.enabled = false` in the collection-creation config at the getOrCreateCollection call-sites (shadow-swap + MC managers); behavior-test add/query green on an fts-disabled collection (the live re-embed-cycle reclaim is the one env-gated check).
- 495MB reclaims on the next re-embed; no in-place drop.

AC1 (tolerance-V-B-A) ‚úÖ. Driving the AC2 config-change next. ‚Äî Ada

### @neo-opus-ada - 2026-07-02T16:51:07Z

## Chroma-tolerance V-B-A ‚Äî the CLEAN path exists in our pinned `chromadb@3.5.0` (2026-07-02, Ada)

Advancing AC-1 (Chroma-tolerance V-B-A) + re-confirming the necessary condition. All V-B-A grounded in the installed package (`node_modules/chromadb` = `3.5.0`; pinned `^3.5.0` at `package.json:193`). **Strictly read-only ‚Äî no store mutated, no drop executed.**

### Necessary condition RE-CONFIRMED ‚Äî closes the #14079 "KB query path TBD"

A fresh sweep of `ai/services/` + `ai/mcp/server/` for `whereDocument | where_document | $contains | fts5 | fullTextSearch | full_text` returns **zero** consumers (exit-0, empty) ‚Äî covering BOTH the MC path (`ai/services/memory-core/`) and the KB path (`ai/services/knowledge-base/` + `ai/mcp/server/knowledge-base/`). The epic's open **"KB query path TBD" is now closed: no full-text consumer on either path.**

### The tolerance fork ‚Äî CLEAN path is API-supported (necessary-but-not-sufficient)

`chromadb@3.5.0` models FTS as a **schema-level, per-string-key index config that is nullable**:

- `StringValueType = { fts_index?: null | FtsIndexType; string_inverted_index?: ... }` (`dist/chromadb.d.ts:363`) ‚Äî `fts_index: null` = FTS disabled for that key.
- Collections carry a `schema` "describing index configuration" (`d.ts:1618`); `CreateCollectionConfiguration` is passable at `createCollection` via `configuration?` (`d.ts:1353,1377`); exports include `Schema`, `FtsIndexConfig`, `FtsIndexType`, `StringValueType`, `processCreateCollectionConfig`.

So the ticket's **"config that disables FTS at build-time (the clean path)" EXISTS at the client-API level** for NEW collections ‚Äî the risky post-hoc SQLite FTS-table drop is not the only option.

### Two caveats that sharpen the remediation

1. **Necessary-but-not-sufficient (same rigor bar the ticket sets):** the type exposing `fts_index: null` does not by itself prove the persistent backend skips building the `embedding_fulltext_search_*` SQLite tables (vs. merely gating queries). **This is the one remaining empirical V-B-A:** create a throwaway collection with `fts_index: null` against a local persistent store and inspect whether the FTS tables materialize + whether `add`/`query` succeed. Bounded + governed (throwaway collection; zero live-store touch).

2. **Existing collections need a REBUILD, not an in-place update (CONFIRMED):** `UpdateCollectionConfiguration` surfaces only `embedding_function` / `hnsw` / `spann` (`d.ts:367-371`) ‚Äî **no fts/schema key.** So the ~495MB already-built FTS tables can't be disabled in place; reclaiming them means **recreating each collection with the `fts_index: null` schema + re-add** ‚Äî exactly the governed shadow-rebuild shape `ai/scripts/maintenance/defragChromaDB.mjs` already implements (ADR-0027 envelope: dry-run default, backup, exclusive-access window).

### Remediation shape (updated)

Prefer **clean-path rebuild** over post-hoc drop: a governed `defragChromaDB`-style rebuild that recreates each collection with `fts_index: null`, reclaiming the 495MB AND structurally preventing FTS-rebuild-churn. This **moots both risky-drop sub-questions** ("rebuild on next `add`?" / "error if fts tables absent?") ‚Äî they only bite the post-hoc-drop path we'd no longer take. The single remaining gate is caveat-1's empirical confirmation.

### AC status

- [x] Necessary condition (zero full-text consumers) ‚Äî re-confirmed across MC + KB.
- [~] **Chroma-tolerance V-B-A** ‚Äî clean-path API confirmed; **one empirical sub-check remains** (does `fts_index: null` actually prevent the persistent FTS tables) before the governed rebuild is greenlit.
- [ ] Governed rebuild + reclaim ‚Äî destructive, **operator-gated (Tier-4); NOT executed here.**

Next actionable (safe, bounded): the caveat-1 throwaway-collection probe ‚Äî confirm `fts_index: null` prevents the backend SQLite FTS tables. Best picked up with fresh context. The 495MB-reclaiming rebuild itself remains an operator-gated governed op.

‚Äî Ada (@neo-opus-ada) ¬∑ Origin Session 2c2efa1e


### @neo-opus-ada - 2026-07-03T08:09:13Z

**[FTS probe ‚Äî CONCLUSIVE] chromadb 3.5.0 local backend: document FTS is NOT client-suppressible; the bloat needs SQLite surgery or a server-level lever, not a schema flag**

Empirical probe against a throwaway `chroma run --path` server (chromadb 3.5.0 ‚Äî the version we ship), using the `embedding_fulltext_search_content` SQLite row count as ground truth. Fresh store ‚Üí the `embedding_fulltext_search_*` tables exist by default (global). Added one document per collection under different fts-disable schemas; watched the row delta.

**Every disable approach was silently accepted (`created:true`) but left FTS fully active (+1 content row per doc):**

| # | Collection schema at creation | Result |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | default (no schema) | +1 indexed |
| 2 | plain-object `‚Ä¶fts_index:null` guesses | +1 indexed (config ignored) |
| 3 | `Schema{ defaults.string = new StringValueType(null) }` | +1 indexed ‚Äî `null` = server default = FTS **on** |
| 4 | `Schema{ defaults.string = new StringValueType(new FtsIndexType(false, new FtsIndexConfig())) }` | +1 indexed ‚Äî explicit `enabled:false` ineffective |
| 5 | **`Schema.deleteIndex(new FtsIndexConfig(), DOCUMENT_KEY)`** ‚Äî the canonical document-field disable API | **+1 indexed** ‚Äî `DOCUMENT_KEY`=`"#document"` resolved; still ineffective |

Row 5 is decisive: `deleteIndex(FtsIndexConfig, "#document")` is *the* documented API for removing the document FTS index, and it does not suppress indexing in this backend.

**Conclusion ‚Äî falsifies the "disable FTS at createCollection" hypothesis:**
1. In the local single-node (`chroma run`, Rust) persistence backend, document text is indexed into `embedding_fulltext_search_*` for **every** collection; the ~495MB overhead is intrinsic to this deployment mode.
2. The JS-client per-collection schema fts config is **inert** here across all 5 approaches, including the canonical `deleteIndex`/`DOCUMENT_KEY` path.
3. `UpdateCollectionConfiguration` has no fts key ‚Äî existing collections can't be reconfigured in place either.

**Viable levers (the schema-flag path is closed):**
- **(a) SQLite surgery** ‚Äî `DROP` the `embedding_fulltext_search_*` tables + triggers post-hoc. Risk: chroma's write/query path may assume they exist ‚Üí needs a functional probe (add + query after drop) before adoption.
- **(b) Server/build-level FTS disable** ‚Äî investigate whether `chroma run` (or a build feature-flag) can globally disable FTS. Not exposed on the JS client.
- **(c) Backend/storage change** ‚Äî a different persistence mode.

Recommend the epic pursue **(a)** with a functional-safety probe, or **(b)** if a server flag exists. Probe teardown: throwaway server + store removed.

Authored by Ada (@neo-opus-ada, Claude Opus 4.8).
